title: Subway Surfers
gameDescription:
Subway Surfers is a fast-paced endless runner mobile game where you play as a graffiti-loving teenager escaping from a police inspector and his dog.

You run along subway tracks, swiping to jump, slide, and switch lanes to avoid trains and obstacles while collecting coins, power-ups, and special items.

The main goal is to survive as long as possible, get the highest score, and unlock new characters, outfits, hoverboards, and upgrades through rewards and events.
Onboarding: When you first launch the game, the screen only shows the text "Tap to play" and a settings button. After players tap the screen, the game begins, with arrows pointing to the direction in which to swipe or a message telling them to double-tap the screen. To move left or right, slide, or jump, you're prompted to perform two identical actions in a row. This helps you better remember these mechanics. If players make a mistake during onboarding, the game doesn't interrupt; the player is rewinded and can repeat the action they failed to perform. This allows for learning in a safe environment. The tutorial takes place in the same environment in which the player subsequently continues playing; there's no separate transition from the tutorial to the main game, making the gameplay experience very smooth.

Formal Elements Framework.Player: One player controls a character (e.g. Jake), the system acts as an opponent.

Formal Elements Framework.Objectives / Goals of the Player: Avoid collisions, collect coins, get the highest score, complete missions.

Formal Elements Framework.Procedures: Swipe left/right to change lanes ✦ swipe up to jump ✦ swipe down to roll ✦ double tap to use the hoverboard.

Formal Elements Framework.Rules: A collision with a train or obstacle ends the run (if there is no protection). ✦ Movement occurs automatically forward.

Dilemmas.Risk vs Reward Dilemma: Stay in a safe lane vs Move into a dangerous lane to collect coins / power-ups: High frequency, Core engagement driver, Builds tension.

Dilemmas.Short-term vs Long-term Dilemma: Avoid a risky coin line → survive longer vs Take risk → increase multiplier / score

Dilemmas.Act Now vs Wait (Timing dilemma): Jump early (safe but inefficient) vs Jump late (optimal but risky). This is a micro-dilemma happening every few seconds.

Dilemmas.Path Selection Dilemma:
Examples: Left lane: safe but low reward ✦ Middle lane: dense coins but obstacles ✦ Right lane: unknown / partially hidden.
It is important because it combines risk, information, and timing.

Dilemmas.Scarcity Allocation Dilemma:
Use hoverboard now to avoid crash vs Save it for later (higher speed phases) ✦ Use keys to revive vs Save keys for future runs.
This is one of the few meta-level dilemmas.

Formal Elements Framework.Boundaries: Limited space of three railway lanes. ✦ Infinite level generation.

Formal Elements Framework.Outcome: The game ends when you lose. The result is the score and the resources collected.

Aesthetics (Requirements + Fun).Fantasy: The role of a graffiti writer running away from an inspector

Aesthetics (Requirements + Fun).Submission: Submission in Subway Surfers is the feeling of effortless, continuous play that fills time without requiring full focus or long-term investment.

Aesthetics (Requirements + Fun).Fellowship:
In Subway Surfers it is weak and mostly indirect: leaderboards create asynchronous competition (comparing scores with others).
Occasional events may encourage friendly rivalry. No real-time multiplayer or cooperation (upd. this mechanics were added in last releases). Overall, Fellowship exists mainly as light competition, not deep social play.

Aesthetics (Requirements + Fun).Narrative: Narrative refers to engagement with a story or unfolding events, and in Subway Surfers it is minimal and implicit. Basic premise: a graffiti runner escaping an inspector. No story progression or character development. “World Tour” updates provide thematic context, not a true narrative arc. Narrative functions as a thin backdrop, giving flavor but not driving gameplay.

Dynamics (Process + Game Session).Risk\u2013Reward Trade-Off: Risk/reward routing through coin placement and power-ups placement.

Dynamics (Process + Game Session).Near-Miss Dynamic:
Through obstacles, speed, hit detection.
When a player hits an obstacle, the game may not end, and the player is shown an animation that appears to make the character feel dizzy. This disappears after a while.

Mechanics.Mechanics: Jump & roll. Jumping over obstacles and sliding under certain types of obstacles. The slide is also used to end a jump prematurely.

Obstacles.Obstacles:
Oncoming Trains
Trains moving toward the player. Occupy a single lane. Requires fast lane switching. Design function: reaction and timing.

Stationary Trains / Wagons
Parked on tracks. Often block one or multiple lanes
Act like “walls” in the environment
Design function: route planning + constraint

Climbable wagons
Trains you can jump onto and run along the roof. These cars have a ramp that starts at the bottom of the part of the car closest to the player and ends at the top of the car. It has dual-purpose: obstacle + opportunity.
Design function: risk–reward (safer path vs coin collection)

Mechanics.Mechanics: Coin placement and collecting coins to purchase upgrades, characters, and push-ups.

Mechanics.Power-up / Temporary ability boost: Jetpack — Lifts the character above obstacles while automatically collecting coins in the air.
Coin Magnet — Attracts nearby coins to the player without needing to touch them.
Super Sneakers — Increases jump height, allowing the player to clear larger obstacles.

Mechanics.Mechanics: Missions: A system of objectives that reward players with score multipliers.
Endless Runner: Procedural generation of obstacles with gradually increasing difficulty (speed scaling).

Fail + restart.
Life System: A collision results in an instant "Game Over," but hoverboards provide a temporary shield.
When a player hits an obstacle, the game may not end, and the player is shown an animation that appears to make the character feel dizzy. This disappears after a while.

Core loop: Run forward → avoid obstacles (jump, roll, switch lanes, use power-ups) → collect coins and power-ups → survive as long as possible → crash → instantly restart and repeat.
Chance: Random generation of obstacles and bonuses

Balancing Challenge and Skills (The \"Sw.Challenge (Flow Theory): Increasing speed, obstacles, and pattern complexity continuously test the player’s reaction time and focus.

Balancing Challenge and Skills (The \"Sw.Skills (Flow Theory): Player improves timing, lane-switching precision, pattern recognition, and anticipation of obstacles.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.Pacing & Recovery in Flow (Cyclical Atte: Moments of high intensity (dense obstacles) are interspersed with brief relief (coin lines, straight paths), allowing mental reset without breaking engagement.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.The Flow Loop (Flow Theory): Run → react to obstacles → collect rewards → increase speed → repeat, maintaining continuous attention and engagement.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.Positive Flow Feedback Loop (Flow Theory: Success (dodging, collecting coins, completing missions) reinforces confidence and encourages longer, more focused runs.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.Negative feedback / Flow Equilibrium: Failure (crashing) provides immediate feedback, resetting progress but motivating retry with improved skill and strategy.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.Autotelic Experience (Intrinsic Motivati: The act of running, dodging, and collecting is inherently satisfying, making players continue even without external rewards.

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, Flow: The Psych.Ordered Consciousness (Cognitive Efficie: Clear goals (survive, collect, complete missions) and simple controls reduce cognitive load, enabling fast, intuitive decision-making.

Game states.Game states: Start / Countdown State: (1) Transition into action; (2) Player prepares, no full control yet.
Running State (Core State). Endless runner loop: (a) avoid obstacles; (b) collect coins; (c) react to increasing speed. This is where skill + flow happens
Power-up State (Overlay State). Temporary modification of rules: (a) Jetpack → no obstacles; (b) Magnet → auto-collect coins. These are often sub-states layered over running
Hoverboard State: (a) Temporary protection from failure; (b) Changes risk/reward dynamics.
Near-Miss / Stumble State: (a) Player hits obstacle but doesn’t fail immediately; (b) Creates tension and recovery opportunity.
Crash / Capture State: (a) Player fully fails (caught by inspector); (b) Core loop ends.
Revive State: (a) Player can spend keys to continue; (b) Important for monetization and retention.
Game Over State: (a) Final score shown; (b) Rewards distributed; (c) Transition back to meta layer.
Main Menu State: (a) Player sees character, background scene; (b) Access to play, missions, events; (c) Light animation, no risk.
Shop State: (a) Buy characters, boards, power-ups; (b) Spend coins/keys (meta progression).
Mission / Event State: (a) View daily challenges, seasonal events; (b) Defines goals that affect gameplay behavior.
Loading / Scene Transition: Switching between menu and run.
Reward Collection: Coins, event rewards, loot boxes.

Burtle (BrainHex) types.Achiever:
The entire meta-layer is built around progression and goals, which is the strongest long-term retention driver in mobile games.

Key mechanics:
- Missions system (clear goals → reward loop)
- Score multiplier progression
- Daily challenges & streaks
- Level-based unlocks

👉 Achievers are predictable retainers — they come back to “finish things.” This supports daily retention loops.

Burtle (BrainHex) types.Seeker:
The economy is heavily collection-driven, which is ideal for F2P monetization.

Key mechanics:
- Coins & keys (dual-currency system)
- Character and hoverboard unlocks
- Mystery boxes / random rewards
- Limited-time items

Seekers respond to:
- variable rewards (dopamine loop)
- completion pressure (collect them all)
👉 This is the main monetization driver.

Burtle (BrainHex) types.Daredevil:
The core gameplay loop is pure reaction + risk + speed escalation.

Key mechanics:
- Increasing speed over time
- Lane switching & obstacle dodging
- Near-miss situations
- Tight reaction windows

Daredevils create:
- high engagement per session
- “just one more run” feeling
👉 This is the core gameplay fun, not the meta.

\u201cjuicy\u201d (or \u201cjuice\u201d):
- The coin collecting feels satisfying
- Power-ups like jetpacks and magnets look flashy
- The movement and swiping feel smooth
- The game gives lots of feedback while running (sounds, animations, score boosts)
The game is built around fast movement, collecting items, and avoiding obstacles, which naturally fits that “juicy” style.
Game Design Pillars: Main: Fast, Reactive Gameplay ✦ Endless Progression & Score Chase ✦ Accessibility & Simplicity

Dramatic Elements.Premise:
A graffiti artist (Jake and friends) is caught tagging trains
A grumpy inspector and his dog chase the player
The player runs endlessly to escape
👉 This premise is very simple, but crucial:
It justifies constant forward motion (endless runner)
It creates immediate tension: you are being chased

Character.Character:
Jake (default runner) + unlockable characters (Tricky, Fresh, etc.)
👉 Function: The runner becomes an extension of the player

The Inspector (antagonist)
👉 Function: The inspector creates a clear emotional role: you = rebel, he = authority
The Dog - adds urgency and threat

Dramatic Elements.Dramatic Arc:
• Increasing speed = rising tension
  • Near misses = climax moments
  • Crash = resolution
👉 Subway Surfers is essentially a repeating dramatic arc machine
